result:
TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, and ordered — it establishes a handshake, guarantees delivery, retransmits lost packets, and preserves sequence. Higher overhead.

UDP is connectionless and fire-and-forget — no handshake, no delivery guarantee, no ordering, no retransmission. Lower overhead, lower latency.

Use TCP for: web (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2), SSH, email, file transfer — anything where correctness matters.

Use UDP for: DNS, video/voice streaming, gaming, DHCP — anything where speed matters more than perfect delivery.

Note: HTTP/3 runs on QUIC, which is built on UDP but adds TCP-like reliability in userspace.

TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, and ordered — it establishes a handshake, guarantees delivery, retransmits lost packets, and preserves sequence. Higher overhead.

UDP is connectionless and fire-and-forget — no handshake, no delivery guarantee, no ordering, no retransmission. Lower overhead, lower latency.

Use TCP for: web (HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2), SSH, email, file transfer — anything where correctness matters.

Use UDP for: DNS, video/voice streaming, gaming, DHCP — anything where speed matters more than perfect delivery.

Note: HTTP/3 runs on QUIC, which is built on UDP but adds TCP-like reliability in userspace.
